Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd set aside time to study, maybe plan for a few hours in the evening, but I rarely stuck to it strictly. Most weeks I'd end up doing about 15 hours total, but spread out randomly whenever I could fit it in. I'm 28 and I've learned that rigid schedules don't work for me—I just go with the flow and trust that I'll get things done eventually.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd have quiet weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming before a deadline. It wasn't ideal, but deadlines were really the main thing that got me moving. Regular study times sound nice in theory, but life just doesn't cooperate with that for me.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline coming up or I'd get a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I missed anything important, but I wasn't one to browse around the LMS for fun. If there wasn't something specific pulling me there, I probably wouldn't think to log in for days at a time.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like a chore I had to remember to do. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, usually when assignments were piling up, but once that pressure eased, so did my logins. It just never clicked as part of my daily routine.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since I learn best by reading and writing things out. I appreciated when lecturers posted clear PDFs or structured notes I could download. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too, especially when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of but needed answers to.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos, to be honest. I'd skim through them or skip them entirely because watching takes so long and I can read the same content much faster. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped unless they seemed directly relevant to an assignment. If something felt like busywork, I just didn't have the energy for it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or to understanding something I actually needed to know. If it felt practical and efficient—like reading a clear summary instead of watching an hour-long lecture—I was more likely to engage. I don't have patience for things that feel like padding.
Q8: Optional activities always felt skippable to me, like they were nice-to-haves rather than essentials. Required ones got done because they had to be done, but optional stuff? I'd look at it and think, 'I could do this, or I could not, and nothing bad will happen.' So I usually chose not to, especially when I was feeling low on motivation.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything and studied offline constantly. I'd print out readings, make handwritten notes, and work through material without being connected to the LMS at all. A lot of my actual learning happened with a notebook and pen, away from the computer, where I could focus without distractions.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes or rewriting key concepts by hand, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also sometimes discuss course content with a friend over coffee, working through difficult ideas together. The LMS only saw me when I logged in, but my learning happened in a lot of other places.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd download a PDF or a set of slides once, then never open them in the LMS again because I had my own copy. I preferred working from downloaded files because I could annotate them, organize them my way, and access them without needing internet. One download, weeks of studying—but the system only saw that single access.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engaging felt harder, usually when I was feeling sad or just emotionally drained. I've been going through some personal stuff this year, and sometimes I just couldn't muster the energy to care about coursework. During those times, even logging in felt like too much effort, let alone actually participating.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. When I was tired or overwhelmed by other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but 'later' kept getting pushed back. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, family things—definitely ate into my study time and made me less present online.
Q14: Oh, definitely. Sometimes I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd read the same paragraph three times without absorbing anything because I was distracted or just not in the right headspace. Being online didn't mean I was actually learning anything in those moments.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt performative and draining. Even when I knew participating would help my grade, I'd put it off because writing something for others to read felt like so much effort when I was already feeling low. I'd eventually do it, but always at the last minute and with minimal enthusiasm.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't do much at all—they were the main force pushing me to engage with the LMS. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly become very active, cramming everything in. It wasn't healthy, but it worked well enough to get my assignments done.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Good feedback motivated me a bit, but honestly, by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally to the next thing. I didn't go back and revisit materials based on feedback very often. It felt more like a report card than a learning tool, if that makes sense.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which suited me in some ways but also let me drift. Without mandatory attendance or strict participation requirements, I could get away with minimal LMS engagement. The structure gave me freedom, but maybe too much freedom for someone like me who struggles with self-direction when motivation dips.
Q19: The gamified tasks they introduced were actually kind of helpful—they made some activities feel less tedious and gave me small goals to hit. But overall, the course design assumed students would be proactive, and I'm not always that person. Clearer pathways or more structured checkpoints might have kept me more engaged throughout.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe even watching videos or reading things, but not actually producing work because the motivation isn't there. High activity doesn't mean high productivity. Sometimes you're just going through the motions without the energy to actually complete assignments.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I had low attendance and didn't log in much, but I still did reasonably well because I studied effectively offline. My exam scores and assignment grades were decent because I put in the work—just not in ways the LMS could track. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how much effort you put in offline. They miss the reading I did from printed notes, the thinking I did while walking, the conversations with classmates. They capture presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos—it's about whether the material is actually sinking in and whether you're thinking critically about it. For me, engagement is mostly internal.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I've been feeling sad and disconnected this semester, or that I've been dealing with personal issues that made it hard to show up consistently. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with my notebook, working through concepts alone. It captures a shadow of my engagement, not the full picture of what I actually experienced.
